quick_skim.title: HPCCFA: Leveraging Hardware Performance Counters for Control Flow Attestation
quick_skim.abstract: Trusted Execution Environments (TEEs) allow the secure execution of code on remote systems without the need to trust their operators. They use static attestation as a central mechanism for establishing trust, allowing remote parties to verify that their code is executed unmodified in an isolated environment. However, this form of attestation does not cover runtime attacks, where an attacker exploits vulnerabilities in the software inside the TEE. Control Flow Attestation (CFA), a form of runtime attestation, is designed to detect such attacks.   In this work, we present a method to extend TEEs with CFA and discuss how it can prevent exploitation in the event of detected control flow violations. Furthermore, we introduce HPCCFA, a mechanism that uses HPCs for CFA purposes, enabling hardware-backed trace generation on commodity CPUs. We demonstrate the feasibility of HPCCFA on a proof-of-concept implementation for Keystone on RISC-V. Our evaluation investigates the interplay of the number of measurement points and runtime protection, and reveals a trade-off between detection reliability and performance overhead.
quick_skim.llm_evidence_en: static and runtime attestation as mechanisms for establishing trust in TEEs

quick_skim.llm_tldr_en: Develops a hardware-based control flow attestation method to establish and verify trust in remote execution.
quick_skim.llm_tldr_cn: 开发了一种基于硬件的控制流证明方法，用于建立和验证远程执行中的信任。
